Everyday Marketing — BUSM 304

What I Learned About Marketing on a Bus Ride

Meredith T. Villaluz Spring 20264 min read

This week, on my way home from my internship on TheBus in Oahu, I noticed something about marketing. I took my seat and saw the entire ceiling covered with local business ads. At first, they just looked like decorations. Looking more closely, I realized there was a plan behind each ad's placement.

A Commute Turned Classroom

These businesses use the time people spend on the bus to get their brands noticed. My boring commute became a real marketing lesson. I started to see ideas from class in everyday life — it made me think more about how ads work and how marketing shapes the spaces we move through every day.

Usually, I would ignore these ads, but this time I started looking at them using what I learned from Chapter 1 of Kotler and Armstrong's Principles of Marketing.

Applying Chapter 1 in Real Life

I saw that each ceiling ad was a market offering — a mix of products, services, and experiences meant to meet a need or want. The brands weren't putting up ads at random. They targeted the needs, wants, and demands of daily bus riders in Oahu.

Needs are basic things people feel they lack. Wants are shaped by culture and temperament. When people can pay for what they want, those wants become demands. I also learned these companies want riders to see that what they offer is worth more than its price — creating customer satisfaction. If not, it could lose that rider's business and their lifetime value.

"The best ads on TheBus didn't list features. They spoke to what commuters actually needed."

The Bigger Lesson

Noticing the ads on TheBus made me realize how marketing concepts apply directly to our surroundings. It showed me that the lessons from class go beyond theory — they shape how businesses and people interact in everyday life. My daily commute became a reminder to look for marketing in action wherever I go.

Kotler Chapter 1 — Concepts Spotted on TheBus
Needs
Basic things riders feel they lack
Wants
Shaped by culture & personality
Demands
Wants backed by buying power
Value
Worth more than its price
